data.title: How The AI Debt Supercycle Is Reshaping Credit Markets
data.description:
By Ashok Bhatia, CFA

Anu Rajakumar: Artificial intelligence isn't just a stock market story anymore. It's become one of the biggest borrowers in global bond markets, with an AI-driven financing wave sweeping through credit. How sustainable is this AI debt supercycle? What happens if the trillions earmarked for data centers, chips, and power infrastructure don't deliver the returns investors are banking on? How should fixed-income investors think about the risks and opportunities of AI alongside the broader outlook for bonds in 2026?

My name is Anu Rajakumar, and today I'm joined by Ashok Bhatia, Chief Investment Officer and Global Head of Neuberger Berman's Fixed Income. Together, we're going to unpack what's behind the AI debt supercycle, where we see the most compelling opportunities, and what the biggest potential missteps might be for bond investors.

Ashok, welcome back to the show.

Ashok Bhatia: Hi, Anu. Great to be back.

Anu: Before we plunge into AI, I do want to set the scene for our listeners. We've just started a new year, Ashok. What's your outlook for fixed-income markets overall, and how are you and your team positioning portfolios for that environment?

Ashok: Well, I think, just to start, maybe I'll spend a few minutes, just real quick, on macro, rates, credit, just some high-level thoughts from us. I think macro is relatively straightforward. We think the US will grow 2.5% this year, so that's up a little bit, up a couple of tenths from where 2025 likely ends up. We think the unemployment rate will continue to rise modestly. The unemployment rate in the US right now is about 4.4%. We think it'll go up to about 4.8%, so a moderate rise from here.

I think, importantly, we think inflation - and this may be where we differ from consensus the most - we think inflation will be well-behaved this year. Core inflation in the US is right now around 2.5%, 2.6%, and we think that's where it will end up at the end of 2026. Importantly, we do think that, ex-tariffs, there's a fair amount of disinflation in the system. If we were to strip out the tariff impact, we think the US will end this year pretty much on top of the Fed's goal of 2% inflation.

One other quick thing to add is, though, we do think this year has a lot more tail risks than last year. There's tail risks about a lot of the dynamics of what we're going to talk about, AI; how does that impact the labor market? What could that mean for upside risk and the unemployment rate? As well as some of the policy announcements coming out of DC just in the last few days that create a lot more of that tail risk about outcomes and how things behave this year.

What does that mean for positioning bond portfolios? I think for rates, we are relatively positive on bonds. We think rates can fall in the US, but I think, importantly, for this year, we're seeing an expanded opportunity set outside of US interest rates. European rates, Canadian rates, even Japanese rates, that's taking up a little bit more of our risk budget. We're more neutral on the yield curve. We think the yield curve can steep and flatten a little bit, but there's not a great opportunity on the yield curve. Given our view on inflation, we've reduced significantly our positions in inflation-protected securities.

Then, last but not least, and this probably will lead us into the discussion, on credit. We've been really emphasizing, even more than last year, exposures to financials, to names we really feel have the minimal amount of downgrade or default risk. It means less industrials, less cyclicals. It means more emerging markets. It means owning a much more diversified number of names than we have in the past. I think that just speaks to, although we think the environment is pretty good, we assess that there is this higher level of tail risk, and we're trying to protect portfolios if we do get one of those outcomes.

Anu: That's a great start. Thank you, Ashok. Maybe just building on from that and staying on credit markets. Through 2025, we saw this wave of AI-related debt issuance from the hyperscalers—I believe that was well over $100 billion—really, that's meant to fund these data centers and chips and power. Why don't we talk a bit about what's driving the AI debt supercycle in investment-grade credit? Really, one of the most important questions, I think, is, will this current pace of issuance be sustainable?

Ashok: Yes. This is one of the big topics, obviously, in the bond market to end last year. Maybe just to take a quick step back. If we look at 2020 to 2024, what we call the AI hyperscalers, these are the Metas, the Googles, the Amazons, the Oracles, the folks that are really building these big data centers and building compute power, that group was issuing about $30 billion of bonds a year over that period.

Last year, as you noted, it actually went up to $120 billion. I think, importantly, that all happened really in the second half and even in the fourth quarter of last year, so very back-end loaded, so four times the issuance last year. This year, we're expecting about north of $200 billion, might be up to $225 billion, so close to a doubling of the pace of what we saw last year.

I think on why it's happening, it's basically the companies that are doing this decided that they can't do it out of cash flow anymore. You had a number of these issuers that what they wanted to invest, either in their internal AI investments, in terms of helping build out power centers, purchasing chips, they were able to fund it internally from cash flow. The numbers have gotten to be both too big for some issuers to do it just out of cash flow. Also, for shareholder returns, some of these companies have basically decided they need to put some leverage in the system for shareholder returns.

It really is, I think, about -- the why of this is the scale of the investments and how that's interacting with companies' underlying cash flow as well as their capital plans.

Anu: Sure. One of the terms I've been hearing about is circular financing, and so I wanted to see if we could talk a bit about that in this context. Could you explain what circular financing actually means, and whether there is a concern that investors should be thinking about as the AI buildout continues?

Ashok: Yes. Maybe I can take that question in a couple of directions, if you don't mind. There is one element of, I think, what the market calls circular financing, which is, say, a company decides to buy Nvidia chips. Then Nvidia is investing in, say, the hyperscaler, or in an OpenAI, or an Anthropic or such. There's that level of basically selling a good, and then that revenue comes back to the other company in terms of a cash flow. There is some of that going on.

I do think it is a concern in the sense that the credits that we invest in -- and I think this is important for a bond investor. The outcome of AI, I think, who is going to be the winner, which company is going to be a winner, is it even going to be the hyperscalers, or are the winners going to be companies we haven't even heard of? There's a lot of open questions.

I think we're trying to approach this with a lot of humility, but I think the one thing we do know is that all we as bondholders will get back is par. That's the most. We will absorb whether if a company is doing poor investments, or is not the winner, or the winner is these companies that haven't even been created today. We, the bondholders, are not going to get the benefit of that. That is going to go to a broadly diversified equity portfolio.

We are very sensitive to the risks that are being created by all of these linkages that if one -- and I look at it a little bit like if you're doing one of these Jenga puzzles, and it gets higher and higher, and then you start to try to put the last piece on, and it gets so intertwined that one thing can set off a stronger wave of issues. That is a fear of ours, and it is partly this circular financing that we just talked about.

I think the other issue, though, that as a bond investor we're sensitive to is in addition to just issuing unsecured debt, which some of these companies are doing, they're also doing structures with leases. I think Meta is an example of this, where at the margin, they've issued a little less debt, but what they have done is commit to long-term leases related to data centers and other investments.

A lease payment has a little bit different treatment if companies ever go into bankruptcy and such, but a lease payment is still an obligation of the company. Leverage is coming into these companies, not just by the corporate unsecured debt that we all quote, but by these lease payments. The leverage increases in these companies has been much more significant for a number of them if we were to factor that in. Definitely some risks, and circular financing is one of them.

Anu: Maybe, actually, staying on that topic of these complex financing structures, are there other challenges that you're really watching, whether that's physical bottlenecks, power and grid capacity, or are there other things that really could show up as credit risks that maybe weren't on your radar a few months or years ago?

Ashok: Yes, there are -- and we've spent a lot of time reviewing this. Picking up on something you just said, this is not just talking to our analyst that covers Alphabet, or Amazon. It's also talking a lot and increasingly to our structured finance and our securitized team. Because what we're seeing is we're seeing this issuance, and if you were to disentangle it, what is the security of a bondholder?

The structures vary an awful lot. The structures can involve the security of a lease payment from the company that's issuing it. It can involve a promise from the company that, at minimum, they'll pay you back par. You might lose some interest, but you'll get your principal back at the end of the day. It also can involve construction financing. Some of these deals now are basically financing in order to build a big data center. Then we also start to get into the issues of the security. Some of these will have kind of a mortgage or a security interest in, say, the building and the infrastructure. Not typically a security interest in the chips that are being put into these data centers, but the physical building and/or the land.

Why is this happening? It's because issuers are both trying to manage this and effectively convert debt obligations into lease payments, which helps their debt metrics, but also because they're optimizing the cost of capital. If you, as an issuer, can get a little bit lower cost of capital by giving some of these guarantees or a security interest in the property or the center, it can involve a lower payment. I think that one point is that there are stronger structures that are being created and weaker structures. I think time will tell, but in our experience in the bond business, strong structures and strong credits, maybe to state the obvious, those things are where we're going to navigate our investments to.

The other thing that I think is increasingly coming in, and we're recording this midweek, but we've seen some headlines now about energy and who should the US consumer, how should they be thinking or paying for the energy uses of these facilities. That's a linkage, too, that we're paying a lot of attention to as well.

Last thing I'll say on this is that as we think about 2026, and we were talking about tail risks at the start, the Trump administration is spending some time on initiatives in housing and really things on this sort of affordability. I don't think it's a crazy idea to think that over the course of this year, AI and these investments might get to be a bit more political than they are today. That could be good. It could also be a negative, and that's something we're very sensitive to.

Anu: Absolutely. You spoke about these varying structures and securitization types. I was wondering, when you're looking at all the different companies who are doing this, where a lot of the attention is on the hyperscalers, but there's also many other types of companies, too, what signals really help you separate the more durable credits from the more vulnerable ones?

Ashok: When we're investing our portfolios, and I would say on the hyperscalers, we're breaking it into two categories. In the bond market, we have the, what I'll call, super-high-quality names, and we'll call that the Alphabets, the Amazon, those types of issuers, so cash-heavy. They have so much cash, they couldn't even spend it all if they wanted to. The credit spreads reflect that. A credit spread on one of those types of names might only be 50 basis points off of US treasuries, which in our world, extremely low, extremely tight. We're basically saying in a lot of portfolios, they're great credits, but we can find better uses of the capital.

Then you have some of the other names, and it can run from an Oracle, a Meta, a little bit more on the extreme side, say, a CoreWeave, some of the operators of these data centers. I think that's something I didn't speak about, but the other element of this is people who operate the data centers, they're also issuing debt. Those are ones where credit spreads can be 150 to 200, if not more, basis points. To put that into context, that's a pretty healthy spread for the ratings and overall credit profile of these names.

Those ones, we're being probably a little bit more defensive than I suspect maybe others, but we're certainly being more defensive on those names because we do think some of the tail risks I spoke about, about structure and interlinkages of the business, we've just made the decision that we can own just a little bit of this debt and put the capital, again, to work in some areas of the market we think are a little bit better.

The last thing I'll say is I do think 2026, it's hard to think we're going to go through this whole year without seeing a little bit more about who's winners, who's losers, what are the returns on capital on AI? There's obviously a lot of discussion about which model is better, and is that going to be what determines the winners in this? I think this is an area where, again, coming back to the most we can get back is par, and we do assess that there is, from a bondholder perspective, above-average tail risk in this area, and that led us to the decision to focus capital in maybe some other areas.

Anu: I really like how you're bringing it back to the basics as bond investors. That's really important. Geographically, how does the story change when you move beyond the US, including the emerging markets? This is, of course, a global AI story. How do you view that?

Ashok: Well, the short answer is, I think and we think it makes emerging markets -- which emerging market fixed-income had a very strong year last year after a number of years of seeing client outflows and pressure on returns. Partly, it was the very strong dollar. It was partly the EM countries generally having to hike rates faster and more because they saw more and quicker inflation pressure.

There's a good tailwind, we think, behind emerging market investments. I think AI and some of these uncertainties about AI, they, at the margin, can help emerging markets

When we think about opportunities, and the opportunity set in the US compared to the rest of the world, I do think what's happening with this AI issuance is also increasing the attractiveness of non-US exposures. That's for whether you're a global investor, or whether you're a US-based investor.

I also think -- and this just comes back to tail risk, and I'm going to go completely off the question here. When we look at the unemployment situation in the US, one of the things that-- we see a scenario this year where we actually see pretty good growth, 2.5%, again. We think the unemployment rate is only going to rise to 4.8%, so a pretty moderate rise. We also see a situation, a tail risk, where that unemployment rate goes up a fair amount. That would be pretty unusual to see strong growth, but also a much faster rise in the unemployment rate.

A lot of that story would be that the US this year would end up seeing a lot more frictional unemployment. The idea here is, yes, the economy is good, but we have people that aren't in the right match for a job. The example of someone who has got a lot of skills programming a computer, but the jobs are more in healthcare. There's this mismatch that leads to this bigger rise in unemployment than maybe you'd expect given the growth outlook.

I say that because, coming back to emerging markets, those are issues that you don't have to worry about as much in emerging markets. You might have other issues, but I think EM, in addition to some of the diversification, we do think in portfolios, it can help protect from some of the tail risk scenarios that are out there for this year.

Anu: Great. Lovely. Thank you. Last question for you. We've been talking today about how companies are using AI and funding it, but what about us, the investment community? How are Neuberger's own investment teams putting AI to work, whether that's integrating research, analyzing data, or making day-to-day portfolio management decisions?

Ashok: I'll hit on two. I think the biggest impacts we've seen have been on, quote-unquote, vibe coding. Just as an example, we wanted to build a tool to help us with some - helping build portfolios. What our quant team had estimated would be in the old way of getting some resources on the programming and app development side, there would have been a six-month project. We got done in a couple of weeks using vibe coding. What shocks me is that was now, at this point, almost six months ago or seven months ago. I can only imagine how much faster it would be today. That has been one big use on the investment side.

I think one point that we're all realizing a little bit is when we started thinking about AI here, we thought it would probably help with our operations a little bit more than the investment side, first of all. Where it's actually been more impactful has been the investment side near term. The other area I'd hit on is research. It is helping create leverage for our folks who can spend more time on the more difficult questions and effectively use AI to do more of the data work and what I'll call the low-level work that is part of an analyst's job and make someone more productive.

Our goal for this year is actually to use AI tools to help identify portfolio adjustments that we can make, that portfolio construction. We're doing a fair amount right now, haven't implemented any of it, but ways that we think we can bring it into the portfolio construction process more. I'm cautiously optimistic on how that will work out.

Anu: Terrific. Thank you very much for those thoughts, Ashok. I can't let you go without a quick bonus question. For our listeners who want to step away from markets for a moment, is there a book, a podcast, or a TV series that you've enjoyed recently that you'd recommend? I'd love to know why that particular choice resonated with you.

Ashok: Okay. That's a good one. I think there's two books that come quickly to my mind, so maybe I'll take the liberty of two. A book I really liked reading just recently, just for pleasure, is called The Wide Wide Sea. I don't remember who wrote it, but it's basically about James Cook and exploration of a lot of the South Pacific back in the 18th century. To me, I just really enjoyed it as just thinking about what life would have been like back then.

The other one I'll say, which I get asked a fair amount by - well, not fair amount, but sometimes by some of our younger folks, people who have just joined the firm, like, what are some good books to read about the markets and finance? The one I always come back to, which may be getting a little dated now, but The Alchemy of Finance by George Soros. I always tell people, if there's one book you want to read about the markets, and you can only read one and how markets work and challenges your thinking, it's that book. One for the pleasure reading and one for the work reading.

Anu: Excellent. Well, it sounds like you need to take a trip to the South Pacific soon as well.

Ashok: Yes, no. Well, let's hope we have a quiet year, and I'll be able to do that.

Anu: Lovely. All right. Well, thank you for being here today. We spoke about a lot of stuff in this episode. We spoke about the outlook for 2026. Ashok talked about the solid US growth of about 2.5%. Inflation looks like it will be well-behaved this year. We talked about the expanded opportunity set outside the US and including in the emerging markets. We also noted there are a number of tail risks to be cautious of. One note that you made that I wrote down, which was that there was an expectation that the pace of issuance in 2026 might double from what it was last year to about $200 billion, $225 billion, which is incredible. We spoke about complex structures, circular financing, and where there could be real-world bottlenecks, especially in power and grid capacity, which are really all key to understanding where the AI-related credit risk could show up. Then finally, we talked about how we at Neuberger are using AI in research, through vibe coding data analysis, and importantly, we're looking to enhance portfolio construction using AI as well.

Hopefully, I caught all of that, Ashok. Was that a good summary?

Ashok: No, that was a great summary. Thanks for having me on again, Anu.

Anu: Absolutely. No, it's great to have you.

Ashok: Thank you.

data.title: USA Compression Partners: The J-W Power Acquisition Addresses Two Key Challenges
data.description:
Thesis

USA Compression (USAC) is expected to close out 2025 with what should amount to a neutral free cash flow position after paying its hefty distribution. This is a large accomplishment for a firm that has historically run in the red following distribution payments.

I last provided an FCF analysis of the company in July of 2025. Since then, the company has slightly improved EBITDA guidance while also trimming capital expenses. However, 2026 appears to be positioned for a step change improvement in free cash flow.

This improvement stems from the acquisition of J-W Power Company. The deal was struck at accretive terms that not only improve the company's FCF profile but also aid in the company's deleveraging strategy. As a result, I expect distribution growth can be a real prospect in 2026.

These factors, as well as a positive outlook for natural gas compression, give me confidence in maintaining a BUY rating for the company.

Previous Coverage

In July, I provided an assessment of USAC's FCF profile (shown in the table below). Since then, the company has slightly improved its full-year EBITDA projections, as well as modestly trimming its CAPEX guidance. In total, after the first nine months of the year, the company appears on track to achieve my projection of a neutral FCF profile.

The new EBITDA guidance of $615 million at the midpoint bodes well for investors who may have been worried about the distribution coverage. The company that has consistently maintained its distribution rate for nearly 10 years now also has balanced its budget to ensure this payment is financially secure for its unit holders.

More importantly, this neutral position should allow the firm to stabilize its debt position. As shown below, the company's debt position has stabilized in recent years as the company's earnings have come more in line with the large distribution.

Combined, these two factors give USAC a stable financial base as it begins to execute on an accretive acquisition of J-W Power. I believe this deal will be very beneficial to unit holders.

Digging Into Why This Acquisition is Transformational

The J-W deal closed on January 12th. Following the transaction, USAC added 800,000 horsepower, bringing the company’s total fleet size to a combined 4.4 million horsepower. In doing so, USAC has effectively closed the gap in fleet size with peers Kodiak Gas Services (KGS) and Archrock Compression (AROC).

This deal was struck on very strong terms for USAC unit holders. The deal was valued at 5.8x 2026 EBITDA, making the deal one of the cheaper M&A deals in this space. The deal was funded through a 50/50 split between equity and debt.

This structure allows USAC to accomplish several key objectives. The primary concern of investors is usually how a deal becomes cash flow accretive.

The J-W assets are expected to generate $155 million in 2026 EBITDA, boosting the combined entity’s EBITDA generation by 25%. In exchange, the company must bear the cost of $430 million in new debt as well as $38.5 million of increased distribution costs.

As shown in the table below, the deal leaves a significant chunk of unallocated FCF available for USAC. It is likely that a small portion of this remaining FCF will be used for maintenance CAPEX, but it remains evident that USAC will have significant options going forward that it could not have previously achieved on its own.

Acquisition EBITDA $155 million
New Debt Financing Cost ($30 million) - 7% financing borrowing rate
New Unit Distribution Cost ($38.5 million)
Net Change In FCF $86.5 million

For those who have followed my coverage of USAC, this goes a long way toward addressing one of my larger concerns about the company. Before this acquisition, the company simply did not have any disposable cash for growth after financing the hefty dividend. As a result, the company was more or less stuck with the status quo.

Now the next obvious question becomes how will the company choose to allocate this newfound FCF. Given that USAC is already the highest-yielding investment option in the compression space, the company may be more inclined to invest these new funds toward growth. While this still remains somewhat speculative, we can be fairly confident that majority owner Energy Transfer (ET) will have a great deal to say about the company’s direction.

The other key accomplishment of this deal is that it is also structured to allow USAC to de-lever below its 4.0x leverage target. On a standalone basis, the debt/EBITDA ratio of the acquisition is approximately 2.8x. As a result, the combined entity’s leverage ratio should fall to approximately 3.85x.

Thus, this deal goes a long way toward addressing many of USAC’s flaws. The company at least now has the option to pursue growth while its debt is in a much more respectable position. ET may favor growing distributions, but capturing growth today can pay distributions tomorrow.

Valuation

USAC now trades at parity with its closest peers. The big three compression providers now trade within a range of roughly 0.5x. One of the more unique features in this space is that despite the similarities in valuation, each compression provider offers vastly different investment objectives for shareholders.

The group ranges from strong growth prospects in AROC to the high-yielding USAC. KGS falls somewhere in the middle of this pack in terms of both growth and dividend payout. Really, this space provides the opportunity for just about any investor, with no particular provider standing out from the pack on the valuation front.

Risks

Despite this being a natural gas compression business, the long-term outlook remains very dependent on future oil activity. Over two-thirds of USAC’s portfolio resides in oil-dominated basins. In these locations, natural gas is more of a byproduct that needs to be managed than a commodity extracted for profit.

Ultimately, oil prices drive margins in these basins, and lower oil prices lead to less activity. With less activity comes lower demand for compression services. Oil markets are largely believed to be oversupplied for the next 12-18 months, making growth in this space somewhat more challenging.

A continued decline in oil prices would likely overcome the increasing gas-to-oil ratios that have been continuously driving more demand for compression assets.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. I previously guided for USAC to achieve neutral FCF following the distribution in 2025. The company is well on track to meet this objective.

2. USAC recently closed on the J-W Power acquisition, which I expect to be very meaningful for investors. The transaction boosts discretionary FCF, making growth and/or distribution growth achievable.

3. The terms of the deal are also accretive to the balance sheet. The sale lowers the combined entity’s leverage to below USAC’s long-term target of 4.0x.

4. By accomplishing both key objectives, USAC has addressed some of its most glaring flaws.

5. USAC remains competitively valued compared to peers, although the potential for growth does not appear to have been priced in quite yet.

6. I continue to rate USAC as a BUY and will patiently wait for further clarification on how the partnership will choose to allocate the newfound cash flows.

data.title: Oppenheimer Upgrade Fuels Relay Therapeutics Rally On Zovegalisib Optimism
data.description:
Thesis: Zovegalisib Builds Momentum

As you know, Oppenheimer upgraded Relay Therapeutics Inc. (RLAY) to Outperform from Perform this week, and the stock saw a considerable boost. Since the upgrade on the 26th, shares are up about 15%. Oppenheimer's reason to be bullish is the growing optimism around its lead PI3Kα inhibitor zovegalisib in breast cancer. Most of you probably know this, but for some clinical background, understand that clinically, zovegalisib is being developed primarily for hormone receptor-positive (HR+), HER2-negative advanced and metastatic breast cancer in patients whose tumors harbor PIK3CA mutations. It’s tipped to be particularly effective after progression on CDK4/6 inhibitors and endocrine therapy. It’s a pretty large population and also a well-defined molecular subgroup of breast cancer with clear biological dependence on PI3K signaling. So inhibition of PI3Kα is the main goal. The firm also set a $14 price target, which would suggest about 88% upside, since shares are currently trading in the mid-$8 range.

They also argued that Relay could benefit greatly if the upcoming data from Celcuity’s (CELC) competing drug, gedatolisib, disappoints in the VIKTORIA-1 study, so that's something to keep in mind for investors going forward. Specifically, analyst Matthew Biegler mentioned that gedatolisib, which is a much broader type of inhibitor, may actually struggle to meet expectations, which could lower the competitive bar for zovegalisib going forward. We will look into that in more detail later, but for now, know that Oppenheimer is in favor of zovegalisib since it has a mutant-selective design, which they hope will provide some clinical advantage.

3Q25

As a quick recap of last quarter, Relay’s last earnings showed us continued clinical execution but also a bit of improved cost control, posting a net loss of $74.1 million, or $0.43 per share. These were improved numbers from the net loss of $88.1 million, or $0.63 per share, in the prior-year period. R&D expenses made up the bulk of that operational spend, coming in at $68.3 million, down from the $76.6 million we saw last year. Now, this was mainly driven by the streamlining of the research organization and just an overall reduced spend on lirafugratinib after its licensing agreement with Elevar. However, the R&D drop was partially offset by higher costs for the ReDiscover-2 and ReInspire trials. G&A expenses fell to $12.1 million from $19.8 million in 3Q24, mainly due to lower stock-based compensation and some employee-related costs. The company also managed to end the quarter with a pretty healthy cash sum of $596 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments. Management has tipped for this sum to fund operations into 2029, which would be a lengthy runway.

Zovegalisib

As for Relay’s pipeline, zovegalisib would be one of their most promising clinical assets. It’s an investigational, allosteric, pan-mutant, and isoform-selective inhibitor of PI3Kα (phosphoinositide 3-kinase alpha). Essentially, PI3Kα is a key signaling protein that is often seen mutated in cancer. So inhibition of this could potentially see some upside. Now, zovegalisib is classified as a mutant-selective, allosteric PI3Kα inhibitor, which means it can bind to a regulatory/non-ATP site on the enzyme and then preferentially block the activity of unwanted oncogenic PI3Kα mutants. The main mutant forms would be those of H1047R, E545K, and E542K, which give you a sense of ‘pan’ inhibition that Oppenheimer is bullish on. The other good thing is that zovegalisib, for the most part, spares the normal, wild-type enzyme. Now, in being selective against mainly the mutant forms, this is a major mechanistic distinction from earlier PI3K inhibitors like alpelisib or gedatolisib, which just inhibited PI3K far more broadly in the hope of suppressing mutant forms. Often this caused dose-limiting toxicities such as hyperglycemia, rash, and gastrointestinal effects. So now, by focusing on mutant PI3Kα, zovegalisib may see the upside of preserving normal insulin signaling and improving the overall therapeutic index. The key goal is to get a deep and sustained pathway suppression in tumor cells as much as possible without causing other harm to the patient.

Moving on from a mechanistic standpoint, once zovegalisib inhibits the downstream PI3K pathway signaling, the result is reduced activation of AKT and mTOR, major central mediators of tumor cell proliferation/survival. Now, since the drug has that mutant-selective targeting, we often see zovegalisib frequently get combined with fulvestrant, an estrogen receptor degrader. You see, endocrine resistance in HR+ breast cancer is often driven by PI3K pathway activation, so by having dual inhibition of estrogen signaling and PI3K signaling, Relay is hoping to restore tumor control and delay progression.

The Data

In terms of dosing, what's good about zovegalisib is that it’s an oral drug, and so far in clinical studies, it has been administered on a continuous twice-daily schedule. The dosing schedule is designed to maintain that sustained pathway inhibition while also minimizing toxicity. And as I mentioned, Relay is able to maintain dosing because of that mutant selectivity. A big plus side is that they are more targeted at the mutant protein forms, and as a result, the observed adverse events have generally been more tolerable than earlier PI3K inhibitors.

So far, zovegalisib is in late-stage clinical development, with a very pivotal Phase 3 trial, ReDiscover-2, evaluating the treatment in combination with fulvestrant in a broad line-up of cancer types, including PIK3CA-mutant, HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer patients who have progressed on CDK4/6 inhibitors. Now, in parallel, it’s also being studied in an ongoing Phase 1/2 ReDiscover trial, which is mainly exploring monotherapy and combination regimens, including the potential of triplets with CDK4/6 inhibitors. This is also set to provide the foundational efficacy/safety data supporting the Phase 3 program. Elsewhere, zovegalisib is being evaluated in a separate Phase 1/2 program for vascular malformations, which goes to show how broad its applicability to PI3Kα-driven diseases is. To touch on the last study briefly, it's a pretty promising sign that zovegalisib isn’t just limited to oncology. It seems Relay is also looking to apply it in genetic disorders where overactive PI3Kα signaling is driving abnormal tissue growth. Again, it’s because of its selectivity to the mutant form of PI3Kα that's allowing this. I'll touch briefly later on how that may expand the TAM.

So far, clinical trial data have shown rather encouraging antitumor activity when they combine zovegalisib with fulvestrant. The reported results so far from Phase 1/2 cohorts showed pretty meaningful objective response rates and median progression-free survival that compared quite favorably with historical outcomes from earlier PI3K inhibitors. This was particularly true in second-line settings. Importantly, however, responses were observed across key molecular subgroups, which included patients with ESR1 mutations and also those previously treated with selective estrogen receptor degraders. So this tells me that zovegalisib can overcome some of the common resistance mechanisms we usually see. From a safety perspective, the data indicate that the most treatment-related adverse events are low to moderate in severity. Also, the events are tipped to be manageable with dose modification or supportive care, which is another promising effect from the treatment being mutant-selective. The table below summarizes the current clinical profile. Keep in mind that some of these studies are ongoing or upcoming.

An Expansive TAM

Because of the treatment range for zovegalisib being so broad, the candidate actually sits in a very large potential global market if approved. Now, the main market would be driven by the overall frequency of PIK3CA mutations across cancers and, in particular, the pretty significant unmet need in other PI3Kα-driven diseases. In metastatic HR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer, PIK3CA mutations occur in roughly 34% to 40% of patients, and Relay internally estimates that the TAM for a mutant-selective PI3Kα inhibitor like zovegalisib, in this specific setting, could exceed $6 billion in major markets. They also mention a particularly promising area being in post-CDK4/6 inhibitor patients who currently have very limited targeted options. Elsewhere, independent market analyses suggest tens of thousands of patients in the U.S. may be eligible annually. Estimates also tip for potential annual revenue in the high-single-digit billions if we see uptake and pricing align with already approved advanced oncology therapies.

I also mentioned that zovegalisib could see a potential market beyond just breast cancer, which is a result of the candidate being able to target other PI3Kα mutations. It would extend the applicability to other solid tumors with these mutations and PI3Kα-driven vascular malformations. There are about 170,000 patients in the U.S. who carry PI3Kα mutations and have few approved systemic therapies. So, for genetic disorders like vascular malformations, the TAM is defined more by the unmet clinical need rather than established pricing benchmarks since there are so few therapies on the market. However, it’s a large patient population with PIK3CA-related overgrowth spectrum and limited alternatives, so I think we can expect a pretty meaningful opportunity here if safety and efficacy are demonstrated. Below is an extract of Relay’s 10K.

Competition Landscape

Now, Oppenheimer is also in favor of Relay since the competition has struggled to get that mutant-specific inhibition. And to touch briefly on that, we often see pricing for approved competitors in the PI3K landscape, such as Novartis’ alpelisib (Piqray), exceed the $200,000 per patient per year mark in the U.S. It’s a rather hefty sum, but one typical of precision oncology agents. Others on the market, such as Roche’s inavolisib/Itovebi, have been forecasted to achieve peak sales in the low-to-mid-billions just in breast cancer alone. So there’s obviously substantial pricing power here for effective, targeted therapies in this segment.

Another big talking point or potential catalyst is Oppenheimer hinting at a possibility in which gedatolisib disappoints in their VIKTORIA-1 study. The firm believes this would shift the market in favor of zovegalisib. Now, both actually target the PI3K pathway in breast cancer, which makes them a good comparison. However, they actually differ fundamentally in how selectively they do so. You see, gedatolisib is a pan-PI3K/mTOR inhibitor, meaning it just broadly suppresses multiple PI3K isoforms and mTOR in the hope that they increase pathway coverage, but in taking this approach, it also raises the risk of off-target toxicities. We usually see those toxicities show up as metabolic and gastrointestinal side effects. Zovegalisib, as you know, is a mutant-selective, allosteric PI3Kα inhibitor designed. Its goal is to preferentially block oncogenic PIK3CA mutations whilst sparing the normal, wild-type enzyme and other important PI3K isoforms.

I also share the viewpoint that this precision is what could allow for more sustained dosing and a better tolerability profile, and general market tailwinds favor Relay if both show promising results. I think this is particularly true with respect to hyperglycemia and rash that have limited earlier PI3K inhibitors. So something to keep in mind here going forward is the VIKTORIA‑1 Phase 3 trial. Celcuity has now completed enrollment in both the PIK3CA wild‑type and mutant cohorts. We should expect the topline results from the PIK3CA mutant cohort to be announced sometime in late 1Q26 or possibly during 2Q26. However, I don’t think a negative outcome here will serve as the catalyst Oppenheimer thinks it will.

Valuation

When it comes to valuation, Relay is a late-stage clinical biotech with minimal current revenue and a balance sheet dominated by its cash position rather than operating earnings. So as you would expect, traditional profitability metrics aren’t really meaningful here since they’re loss-making and pre-commercial. In terms of market cap for Relay, it sits around $1.32 billion after the latest run-up. The company also has an enterprise value of about $752 million, again supported by that large cash sum of $596 million against just modest debt at $33 million. Now, that would indicate that much of the equity value is backed by cash.

On an asset basis, the stock trades at about 2.2x book value, which is just a bit below the healthcare sector median and close to its own five-year average, which tells me the market is valuing Relay primarily on its pipeline optionality, with much of the stock momentum dependent on topline data milestones. In contrast, EV to sales sits at 59x on a forward basis and price to sales at 103x, which looks extremely elevated versus the sector but is very much expected given Relay’s limited licensing revenue so far. As for average daily trading volume, we typically see around 2 to 2.1 million shares, according to the recent volume data.

Looking Ahead

So next for Relay, I see the key near-term catalyst as being the topline Phase 3 ReDiscover‑2 data for zovegalisib in combination with fulvestrant. That's in PIK3CA‑mutant HR+/HER2‑ metastatic breast cancer, and we can expect the data sometime in mid‑2026. A good outcome here should validate the earlier signals of meaningful progression-free survival/response rates. There are also additional updates we might see from the ongoing ReDiscover and vascular malformation programs. This would, of course, include those triplet combination cohorts and proof-of-concept data. Moving to the financial side, Relay should report 4Q25 and FY25 earnings sometime around February 25th, which will give us an update on that hefty cash position and how in line their R&D spending is.

data.title: Why 2026 Could Redefine The U.S. Federal Reserve
data.description:
The Federal Reserve may be entering one of its most consequential periods in decades, as political scrutiny intensifies and long-standing policy frameworks are questioned. Kevin Hebner, Managing Director and Global Investment Strategist at TD Epoch, outlines what could change, the implications for monetary policy, and what it could mean for investors.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: As we head deeper into 2026, the US Federal Reserve may be facing one of its most pivotal years in decades. My next guest says central banking, as we've come to know it, could be coming to an end and be giving way to a far more politically constrained institution. And the stakes for markets are high. Joining us now to break it all down is Kevin Hebner, Managing Director and Global Investment Strategist at TD Epoch.

Kevin, great to have you back on the show.

Kevin Hebner: Hey, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about this intriguing idea, 2026 being a true turning point for the Federal Reserve. Why more so than in other years?

Kevin Hebner: So, it's really about the imperial presidency of Trump. And so, as you know, Trump is not a big fan of checks and balances, and he's breaking lots of norms. And so this affects his relationship with Congress. So he's dominating Congress.

Last year was a record high in terms of the number of executive orders passed, record low for pieces of legislation. With the Supreme Court, you now have a majority of the court is aligned with Trump. Most government agencies now, Trump is in control of. Fed is sort of the last man standing.

And we're seeing, with the Donroe Doctrine in the Western hemisphere, Trump wants to have dominance in that range. So it's really about dominance of this person, everything being aligned with Trump's economic and security agenda. And the Fed is pretty key to that. So, yes, I think this is a key year for the Fed. And it certainly looks like Trump will be having his way, that around May we'll see a clear majority of Fed governors being aligned with Trump's agenda.

Greg Bonnell: Alright, so we have this push, and the Fed independence is being actively tested. Why is this such a central issue for the president?

Kevin Hebner: So, it's important because the Fed has lots of levers. And historically, the Fed has just had responsibility for short-term interest rates. That's been pretty important. But what we've seen, over the last 25 years or so, with the global financial crisis, with COVID, is that the policy levers of the Fed has widened a lot, so-called mission creep, particularly when Ben Bernanke was Chair of the Fed. So they went from short-term interest rates, zero-interest rate policy--ZIRP-- to quantitative easing. The size of the Fed's balance sheet went up 5x.

The Fed set up all sorts of special purpose vehicles, typically to help out-- sometimes bail out-- financial companies, insurance companies, and so forth, helping Wall Street, not helping Main Street so much. And then also a lot of dollar swaps that they offer to other central banks in a crisis when countries outside the US-- this would include Canada-- either need US dollars to import, say, essential emergency goods into the country during crises, or they have US dollar debt that they need to roll over and funding markets have frozen up.

So the Fed's remit has expanded enormously over the last 25 years through a series of crises. And many of these areas are inherently political. How do we help our allies? How do we transfer wealth between Wall Street and Main Street? And so much beyond the historical remit that the Fed has had sort of since 1913, very much focused on narrow, short-term interest rates.

Greg Bonnell: So this idea of mission creep of the Fed, and you're saying some of this mission becomes political, does this leave them vulnerable to this kind of attack on their independence?

Kevin Hebner: I think a lot of it does make them vulnerable because, certainly, affecting short-term interest rates-- and that is a political act. And we have seen even within Fed members-- like Democratic members of the Fed will tend to be more dovish when there's a Democratic president, and vice versa. And similar with Republican. So that's political.

But once you start to get into quantitative easing, a massive balance sheet, special purpose vehicles, and dollar swaps with other countries-- these are all very political decisions. And you have these made by unelected, unaccountable so-called bureaucrats. But many of them are political themselves.

The Chair before Powell, Janet Yellen, she's a fantastic economist. But she was also Treasury Secretary for Biden. She's a very political person. So these things are inherently political. And the White House is very concerned that you have these very political decisions-- the vast majority of Fed staffers donate to the Democratic Party, not to Republican Party-- that these things have become highly politicized, and that they should, as a consequence, be reporting into the White House, not being independent.

Greg Bonnell: So in that sense, I mean, some people might be saying the mission creep justifies the Fed losing some of its independence. But under that scenario, I don't hear them losing any of that mission that they've creeped into. It'll just simply be redirected with a straight line of saying, this is what we want you to do.

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. And I think that the idea is to reinvent the Fed a little bit so the Fed is more focused just on short-term interest rates. The bank supervisory role of the Fed, which became-- which has existed since 1913 but became much more important in 2010 after the global financial crisis-- that that should go, say, back to the Treasury.

Greg Bonnell: Oh, so there would be some changes. There would be some pulling back on their scope.

Kevin Hebner: I think a lot of changes are envisioned to have a narrower, more focused Fed on these issues, and the other functions that the Fed has taken upon themselves over the last 25 years, that those should go to the Treasury or the FDIC or other aspects of the government.

Greg Bonnell: OK, so that's a great backdrop and great stuff to think about in terms of what the Fed might actually get up to this year. I mean, the markets right now are pricing in about two rate cuts. If we do get a Fed that's perhaps more sympathetic to the current administration, doesn't that seem a little low of a number?

Kevin Hebner: Yeah, I think we're seeing more than two cuts. And the base-case view is, GDP growth is 2% plus or minus. Core CPC inflation currently around 3%, goes down to about 2 and 1/2%. So you have an environment which is conducive to the Fed cutting.

I think in May, when we get a majority of Fed governors are aligned with Trump-- and actually, I think around May we get a fifth governor resigning and then replaced by a Republican-- then I think you have a supermajority of Fed governors aligned with Trump, and I think they could make the case that the Fed's funds rate should be below 3%. And so I think we could get three or four cuts, rather than the two cuts that the market has priced in.

Greg Bonnell: Is three or four cuts going to be constructive for the US economy? Could there be any problems with the more aggressive rate cutting path?

Kevin Hebner: Well, it certainly means a lot of liquidity. And liquidity is super important to risk assets. At the same time, what's going on in bank supervision-- so the person responsible for bank supervision, in terms of the Fed governors, Michelle Bowman, she's a Republican. So any policy she wants to put in place has to be approved by a majority of Fed governors. So that becomes a lot easier from May.

So I think we'll see policies from the Fed easing capital requirements and more encouraging policies to have banks lend more. So I think we get more bank lending. We get lower policy rates. And I think we also get a lower US dollar. That's a bit more complex. But you have three sources of liquidity, I think, as a nice tailwind for risk assets and equities this year.

Greg Bonnell: In terms of who's going to be in that Fed Chair when it's no longer Chair Powell, does the market have a favorite? Is there one-- is there someone now who looks like they're in the lead?

Kevin Hebner: Yeah, so prediction markets are trading very actively on this. So right now, there's about a 50% probability on Kevin Warsh. And he's been in previous Republican administrations. 25% probability on Rick Rieder, who's currently head of fixed income at BlackRock. And then another 10% or so on Kevin Hassett, who's currently in the White House.

Greg Bonnell: I think he was just told by his boss, though, they like him where he is. I've been in those positions before where I wanted a different job. They said, we like where you are. We like you where you are, Greg.

Kevin Hebner: Yes. You don't want to be so good that you can't get promoted. But we'll probably know in a week's time, something like that-- because it does take some time to get the person through Senate, and they have to be approved by the Senate to get the job and to get markets ready for it.

But I think, rather than the person, it's important to focus on what are Trump's two key criteria for whoever gets the position? The first one is loyalty. And loyalty is super-important for Trump, because in Trump-- his first term, there are many cases in which he appointed people who looked great on paper, had good meetings with him, but there wasn't a lot of loyalty. And, for example, his trade-- Robert Lighthizer fit that bill. And so Trump, first of all, wants someone who's loyal. And certainly, Warsh and Hassett fit that bill. It's not so clear about Rieder.

The second criteria is competency and how they're viewed by the street. And particularly, the Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent, he has a long Rolodex. He was a hedge fund manager for decades. And so, he will call up his friends in the Street and make sure that whoever ends up getting the nomination is someone the Street likes, because Bessent managing the 36 trillion of treasuries at the Fed and Trump, as well as mortgage securities, everything-- they don't want the yield curve moving up, mortgage spreads moving up and so forth.

So those are the two key criteria. And I think any of the three or four-plus people who are being considered by the Trump and prediction markets are looking at would be totally fine.

Greg Bonnell: We've been talking about the central bank and the path that it's going to be on this year once we do get that new Fed Chair-- more aligned with the Trump administration is the thinking here. You say the next crisis, then, could be much more complicated. Walk me through that.

Kevin Hebner: OK. Well, the next Fed Chair will definitely be more aligned with Trump. That's a certainty. In terms of how this will make the next crisis more complicated-- so we were mentioning before about US dollar swaps. And so, if there is a crisis, the Fed has open US dollar swaps with lots of central banks globally-- Bank of Canada, the ECB, and so on-- because they import stuff from the US, and they need US dollars to pay for it. And also, they need to roll over US dollar debt that they've issued. So, in a crisis, funding markets freeze up and you can't get this. And so the lender of last resort is the Fed.

And so you can imagine that, if we're in a crisis and say, the ECB needs dollars. And so, Trump will say, fine, we'll give you dollars but we want Greenland. Or with Canada, fine, we'll give you US dollars but we want rare earths. We want you doing more for Arctic defense. So it becomes transactional.

And that could draw out. That could take weeks. It could take months. And so, in a crisis moment, time is really important. But if you're dealing with transactions, you're negotiating with presidents, these crises could be worse. And I would expect that there will be more difficulties.

Now, the central bankers of the world realize this, and they're all-- in response to that, they're trying to make sure that they're less exposed to the US dollar. And so, this is something we've been seeing in the last year from central banks across the world, realizing this will happen in the next crisis, to try to be less dependent on US dollar funding.

Greg Bonnell: So the world of central banking getting used to the fact that it's not going to be the same. I know in the work that you've been doing, you've been talking about this idea that we're seeing the end of New Keynesian central banking. In plain English-- I mean, I studied that stuff in university, it's been a long time for me-- what does that actually mean?

Kevin Hebner: So Keynes came to prominence with the Great Depression, and he argued that the Great Depression was prolonged because we tried to rely on market forces to bring us out of it. And market forces didn't work. There was a liquidity trap, and all those sorts of things he worked on. So he said, you need really aggressive policies, including fiscal policy but also certain monetary policies.

And then Ben Bernanke, who's the one who really brought New Keynesianism into the Fed, his PhD thesis was on the Great Depression. And he studied Keynes' writing, and he looked at the monetary transmission that was responsible for the Depression, both starting and lasting so long, really until 1941 in many respects.

And so, Bernanke was an expert on that. And then, when the global financial crisis happened, Bernanke went in with both feet. So, cutting interest rates to 0, increasing the size of the Fed's balance sheet 5x, setting up all these special vehicles to fund out financial institutions that were in trouble. The US dollar swaps with central banks. He did an enormous amount of things, so a massive expansion of the responsibilities of the Fed. This is what New Keynesian is.

And the environment we're in today, where the Fed has become so political and is involved in so many political activities-- and the White House thinks a lot of this should be done by the executive because they are elected, they're accountable, whereas people at the Fed are not. So I think the type of New Keynesian that we had, both during the global financial crisis and COVID, we won't see that again. These responsibilities will be from within the White House.

Greg Bonnell: Another part of your work, you got a chart for us here, drawing some parallels to the era of Richard Nixon. Let's throw the chart up and walk us through it. What are we looking at here?

Kevin Hebner: So the president who, in many respects, is most similar to Trump historically has been Nixon. And Nixon did lots of things-- obviously, Watergate, the Vietnam War-- some good things, opening ties with China. But one thing he's most remembered for by economists is the increase in inflation during the '70s. And a big reason for that is he beat up the Fed Chair-- his name was Burns-- so badly.

Nixon would meet with him weekly and would be screaming at him weekly. And we can see from Burns' diary-- and in the paper we have on these policies, we have a couple excerpts from Burns' diary in which he is worried that Nixon was screaming at him. He could see the veins in Nixon's forehead being extended. And ultimately, Burns was terrified, and he capitulated and gave Nixon the policies he wanted.

So you had monetary policy being much looser than it should be. Inflation soared. We had mortgage rates at 20% in Canada as well in the late '70s, early '80s as a consequence. And so this is what happens when you have a Fed that is making policy in response to a mercurial, emotional president. And this is the big worry, the big risk of a more Fed-aligned or a more Trump-aligned or more Trump-dictated monetary policy.

Greg Bonnell: We've covered a lot on the central bank. I want to get a few more themes in there, including the US midterms later this year. How important are those to the markets?

Kevin Hebner: So, I think there are a number of constraints on Trump, which the-- and probably in the midterms, we'll see the Senate remaining Republican-- so, prediction markets are saying 2/3rd likely-- but 75% chance the House goes Democratic. And so, in that case, there could be some ballot checks on Trump's power. But the House will issue subpoenas, and Trump and the DOJ and his attorney general will just ignore them.

The real checks on Trump-- one is popularity. It's pretty low right now, and for reasons I think you understand. Secondly, his competency. He's very good at announcing things. He's not so great at getting in the weeds and actually making sure things happen.

A third issue for Trump is there are some remaining checks and balances. For example, there are three moderates at the Supreme Court. So he doesn't get his way totally with the Supreme Court. And even there's a couple people in Congress pushing back a little bit.

I think the fourth constraint on Trump is just his age and his health. And we'll have to see how that happens. This is an extremely demanding job. And then I'd say the fifth constraint is the House turning Democratic in November. So it's a bit of a constraint on him, but I'd say it's number five in the scheme of things. And ultimately, the trend we've been seeing towards a more imperial Trump presidency, I'd expect that to continue for the next three years.

Greg Bonnell: We've only got about a minute left. We put everything together we've been talking about-- I know it's a lot. This kind of year, is this a year of risk, opportunity, or both in the markets?

Kevin Hebner: Yeah. So we're constructive on equities. So you're going to have, we think, earnings growth of 6-10%. You're going to have a lot of liquidity. You have valuations in equity markets are a little bit stretched, but only moderately so. So we think there's upside, high-single digit upside for equity markets. And our key theme would be diversification away from concentrated bets in, say, the Mag Seven-- so big tech companies-- and towards other types of companies in the US and markets outside the United States.

Original Post

data.title: Quest Diagnostics: A Continuing Bullish Case For A Leader Among Diagnostic Testing Brands
data.description:
Quest Diagnostics (DGX), the New Jersey-based healthcare services firm focused on diagnostic testing and related solutions, has its next earnings call in a few weeks on Feb. 10, so it hit my radar again for a follow-up, considering it is up around +8% since my July 2025 buy rating and up around +2% since I called it a buy again this past October.

In both coverages, two key bullish drivers were macro demand for diagnostic testing as well as impressive profit margins, so today we will explore some additional angles of this stock and dive into some data from the most recent earnings results that came after my last article.

Thesis Summary

My updated thesis on Quest argues that my prior 2 buy ratings should be reaffirmed yet again, and my continued bullishness is driven by Quest's strong market position in its niche along with demand for clinical testing, despite payor risk, and also a proven model of driving strong margins in its peer group, organic business growth, investment-grade credit ratings, and expected further price upside.

My insights worksheet below shows what categories drove this updated score today:

Actionable Insights

Macro & Sector Outlook

Regarding this niche sector within healthcare, I want to continue echoing the projected macro demand, considering that since my last article, the research firm Research and Markets was cited in a major financial site in November, saying, "The clinical laboratory services market is expected to increase from USD $251.9MM in 2025 to USD $358.6MM by 2034, growing at a CAGR of 4%."

However, there has been potential headwind pressure at the public policy level ever since the US Congress passed PAMA some years ago, aimed at "comprehensive reforms to how Medicare pays for laboratory tests." Back in Q3 remarks, Chairman and CEO Jim Davis remarked, "If Congress does not reform or delay PAMA this year, American labs will be forced to absorb significant payment cuts next year."

Now, rather than get into a lengthy policy debate here, since laws can be changed or new amendments introduced each time a new Congress comes into power, my impression is that despite the stable macro demand for clinical testing solutions, this sector also faces potential downside risk from payor pressure (Medicare, for example). That is a significant business impact, as it directly impacts the amount that firms like Quest get reimbursed for Medicare patients. For this reason, I gave this stock a hold, or neutral view, in this category.

Competitive Positioning

When it comes to competitive positioning, the first notable point to mention is that Quest made the news this month as "it has been named to the Fortune magazine 2026 World's Most Admired Companies list, for the 12th consecutive year," according to a company press release.

I think that is significant for both brand recognition and penetration, driving future upside potential.

In addition, the peer group I selected to compare some metrics against includes what I believe is Quest's main peer, Labcorp (LH), but also firms involved in the clinical/diagnostic testing niche such as Natera (NTRA) and Guardant Health (GH). All 4 peers are in the market cap range of $14B - $33B, so I think they make a decent comparison group.

Notably about Quest also is that it leads this specific peer group in return on equity (TTM), at +14.33%, according to the data below, which I think could add some confidence to bullish investors.

Another impressive metric I think works in Quest's favor within this peer group is that although it did not beat the 5-year price return of the S&P 500, it was 2nd in this peer group in this regard, seeing a +45.7% price return in the last 5-year period shown, well above its key peer Labcorp:

So, based on this impressive momentum, the brand recognition in Fortune magazine, and the strong return on equity, I called it a buy in this category.

Company Growth Factors

As far as company growth, since my last article, the company had posted its Q3 results, which showed organic business growth, with the investor deck comments mentioning that "revenues grew 13.1%, including 6.8% organic growth, driven by broad-based adoption of our clinical innovations, contributions from acquisitions, and growth in our consumer channel."

This ought to provide some momentum heading into 2026, as well as added investor confidence. Already, Seeking Alpha data shows fwd revenue growth expected at +7.1%, which is not far from the sector average.

Further, the Q3 investor deck also had improved top-line guidance for FY25, so in the upcoming earnings results in a few weeks, that will be a key item to watch for as to whether they can meet or exceed this guidance, since they should be reporting for Q4 as well as full-year FY25:

What we can also expect going forward, and I think that could be an upside factor, is further portfolio expansion. For instance, in Q3 remarks, CEO Davis also said that "acquisitions are a key growth driver, and our strategy emphasizes purchases of accretive hospital outreach and independent labs."

Importantly, if we are to consider revenue trends for Quest, we can see in the table below (from the Q3 supplemental pg. 16) that although the demand for Covid testing clearly has fallen drastically in the period of 2022-2024, at the same time there has been an uptick in revenues among both routine clinical testing and gene-based services:

Thus, if this trend were to continue into fiscal FY25 results about to be announced soon, it shows that a firm like Quest has a resilient and diversified portfolio of testing solutions with steady and growing demand and can overcome the spikes and dips caused by a global pandemic.

In my articles on healthcare and nursing-home REITs (such as my coverage of CareTrust REIT), I often point to forecasts expecting growth in outpatient clinics as well as skilled nursing/eldercare facilities, driven by an aging population and demographic population growth in certain US regions, and so I think that could also position Quest favorably to take advantage of demand for routine clinical testing.

For the multiple reasons above, I gave the stock a strong buy in this category.

Profitability Metrics

When it comes to profitability metrics, primarily I want to consider net profit margins among the selected peer group, which clearly show that Quest has led this peer group over 3 years with a profit margin of +9.4%, indicating much more efficiency at driving earnings than some peers:

I think the strong margins can also be attributed to a company strategy of driving cost efficiency. For example, in their Q3 remarks (pg 4), the company said:

Turning to operational excellence, we continue to target 3% annual cost savings and productivity improvements through our Invigorate program. We are deploying innovative automation and AI technologies, including digitizing processes, to improve quality, productivity, and customer and employee experiences.

Given the margins vs. peers and the company's strategic direction, it is going in, but also consensus estimates pointing to a range of 5-10% in YoY EPS growth over the next few years, I think a strong buy in this category is justified.

Balance Sheet Risk

When it comes to getting a sense of balance sheet risk, as a baseline, let's consider that Quest has investment-grade ratings from major credit rating agencies, such as BBB from Fitch and BBB+ from S&P, according to the Quest company website.

From the point of view of Fitch, who actually ended up upgrading this company last April, they said Quest has a "leading position and scale in the competitive U.S. clinical laboratory industry."

I think these solid credit ratings could keep supporting at least a neutral or bullish case among more risk-averse investors but also can continue positioning Quest to get favorable funding terms.

In terms of its debt-to-equity, Quest's D/E is around 0.87, the highest in the peer group; however, it is not a major outlier vs. its closest peer by business type, Labcorp, so I would say the leverage risk in relation to equity is modest:

On the asset side, its balance sheet does not have significant exposure to certain assets that may pose considerable market risk, such as investment securities (fixed-income bonds, equities) or significant illiquid assets like real estate portfolios, like I find with healthcare REITs; however, it does have exposure to a considerable amount of accounts receivable, indicating that it will continue depending heavily on the conversion of those receivables into cash as quickly as possible. So, payor risk exposure is something to consider, which I alluded to earlier.

Nevertheless, I believe the strong credit ratings and modest debt-vs-equity are reason enough to call this stock a buy in this category.

Dividend Outlook

From the point of view of dividend coverage, we should consider that Quest's payout ratio has consistently remained below 50% over the 3-year period shown, with 2 of the peers in this group not even paying a dividend, but also we can see that Labcorp may have a more favorable position here:

My subjective benchmark has been a payout ratio of 75% or less, but I also think it should be in the context of similar peers, so here Labcorp could have more cash wiggle room with a much lower dividend payout ratio.

However, besides the ability to support the dividend, I also think about whether a firm has proven itself in growing it over time, and Quest has done so, with a 10-year dividend CAGR of +7.7% (vs. avg. of 6.9%). We can visualize the steady growth trend in the chart below:

This trend indicates dividend resilience in spite of major market shocks in this period, like global pandemics, inflation spikes, and interest rate hikes, as well as tariff/trade wars in 2025. That type of resilience, I think, is important in my long-term view of a stock, rather than just thinking like a day trader. I often ask, has a company lived through a crisis or market shock, or several, and not only survived but continued to grow? The answer here is yes.

Where the dividend story is less attractive is the fwd div yield being around 1.7%; however, it is actually close to the sector average (1.3%), so it happens to be a sector that investors building a high-yield portfolio may not appreciate.

Nevertheless, putting aside the yield, I gave it a buy in this category due to a reasonable payout ratio and proven growth over time, which I think it can continue sustaining through strong cash flow, considering its fwd operating cash flow growth is expected at +7.3%.

Valuation Metrics

Moving on, let's talk about valuation in relation to future price upside potential, and we'll use my worksheet below that uses peer valuation data and earnings consensus data:

Based on available valuation data from Seeking Alpha that would have been suitable to use, I only compared against Quest's key peer, which is Labcorp, and in this case Quest is slightly undervalued on its forward earnings multiple but slightly overvalued on its trailing price-to-book multiple. The difference is not stark, being +/- 7 to 10%, so it tells me the market expects Quest to likely perform relatively in line with Labcorp. Given that Quest had the best net profit margin in the peer group, which I showed earlier, I actually think this could be a great undervaluation opportunity.

Underscoring that is the fact that my price target implies further +19% upside in this stock price, which I think justifies calling this stock a strong buy in this category.

Technical Charting

Having discussed multiple categories of fundamentals, in this last topic I'll talk about the technical chart I made below:

What we can learn here is that there is no clear buy signal (golden cross pattern) or sell signal (death cross pattern) that appears in this chart, but rather the 50-day and 200-day SMAs are running parallel, and the longer-term SMA is on a steady upward slope. However, the share price does not remain steadily above the 50-day SMA as a support line but is quite volatile.

Therefore, based on my research into this topic over the last several months, I am more inclined to call it a hold at this point if purely looking at this technical trend; however, it does not change the overall rating score meaningfully.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

A key potential challenge to my bullish view that goes beyond payor risk and has not been mentioned so far in this article is the highly regulatory and potentially litigious nature of this sector itself that Quest is in. Such risk can pose future downside risk from a reputational, legal, and cost perspective, something investors should consider.

For instance, an example of this in the past has been the 2024 press release from the California Attorney General revealing a settlement was reached with Quest, who was "required to pay nearly $5 million for penalties, costs, and supplemental environmental projects and make significant changes to its operations and practices at its California facilities," in the wake of a case involving allegations that the diagnostic laboratory company unlawfully disposed of hazardous waste, medical waste, and protected health information at its facilities statewide.

To be objective here, I should also mention that its key peer, Labcorp, in a completely separate case, "agreed to pay $19 million to resolve allegations that it violated the False Claims Act by its submission of false claims to Medicare," according to an official press release by the US Dept. of Justice/US Attorney.

So, I think that such risks, if they occur in the future, could move a stock unfavorably, which poses some downside potential, while a mitigating factor could be that such cases or allegations become only a rare occurrence, statistically speaking.

Conclusion

In conclusion, today's updated thesis showed several reasons why there could be a lot more upside potential in this stock than downside risk, which could favor long-term investors, and the multiple bullish drivers included macro factors and company organic growth as well as impressive profit margins and upgraded company guidance, along with strong credit ratings.

What I think investors and analysts will be looking for in the upcoming earnings results in a few weeks are whether the company can meet or beat its upgraded FY25 guidance, whether the positive EPS consensus will be beat or not, and perhaps further commentary from the firm on additional acquisition/expansion prospects, since I mentioned the company's wording that points to a strategy of acquisition and growth.

As a steady dividend grower over 10 years, with a modest payout ratio, I am also adding this stock to my dividend ideas for the month.

Rating Methodology

Insights by Albert Anthony is the author's research approach that aims to tell a holistic story about a stock, starting with the larger macro/sector overview and narrowing down to a detailed technical price trend, focused on concisely answering questions like what could move the stock in the future, what is its market advantage, and does its valuation make sense in relation to future upside potential.

data.title: PGIM Jennison Focused Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Barron’s Best Fund Families: Ranked #4 for best fund family over the 1-year period ended 12/31/2024, among 48 qualifying fund families based on asset-weighted returns.

Market Review

  • U.S. equity markets ended 2025 with modest fourth-quarter gains, capping a strong year of performance.
  • Renewed concerns around the durability of artificial intelligence (AI) investment and elevated spending on AI infrastructure contributed to market volatility late in the year.
  • Despite valuation pressures, easing monetary policy, moderating but positive economic growth, and ongoing AI adoption supported market strength into year-end.
  • Market leadership broadened as investors rotated from AI-driven growth stocks toward cyclical and defensive sectors, supporting value, small-cap, and cyclical performance.
  • High valuations left limited tolerance for earnings shortfalls, and even in-line results often prompted share price declines.

Sector Performance

  • The Russell 1000 Growth Index advanced 1.1% for the quarter.
  • Health Care, Communication Services, Financials, and Consumer Discretionary were the only positive performing sectors.
  • Meanwhile, all other sectors were negative, with Real Estate and Utilities significantly underperforming the Index.

Fund Performance

For the quarter, the PGIM Jennison Focused Growth Fund returned 0.8%, underperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index.

Barron’s: PGIM Investments ranked 4 out of 48, 8 out of 47, 12 out of 46 firms for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods ended 12/31/2024, respectively. See back page for methodology which takes into account Lipper rankings. Security Contribution

  • The Fund fell modestly short of its benchmark during the quarter. Security selection in Health Care, Consumer Discretionary and Utilities contributed the most to results, while selection in Communication Services and Information Technology detracted. Although sector allocation effects are residual to Jennison’s bottom-up process, they marginally hindered returns this period. An overweight to the Communication Services sector aided performance, while a slight underweight to Health Care detracted.
  • Top performers for the Fund included:
  1. Alphabet (GOOGL) benefited from its strengthening AI leadership following the Gemini 3 launch and increased interest in its custom AI thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) chip.

  4. Amazon’s (AMZN) shares rose in the fourth quarter on renewed confidence in Amazon Web Services (AWS) growth, margin improvement, and the company’s AI opportunity.
  5. Broadcom (AVGO) benefited from growing demand for its AI accelerators and networking chips.
