data.title: AGCO Corporation: Expect This Fruit To Sour (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Many of the companies that I analyze are ones that I try to keep up with on a regular basis. But every so often, a firm will fall off my radar for a time. One such example of this can be seen by looking at AGCO Corporation (AGCO), a rather interesting player in the agricultural industry. Way back in February of 2022, I wrote my last article about the business, calling it a ‘buy’ candidate. But a lot has changed since then. Weak industry conditions after 2023 pushed revenue down rather considerably. Cash flows have taken a hit also. And in all likelihood, we should expect this kind of weakness to continue in the near term. This is why the stock is up only 0.2% since then at a time when the S&P 500 is up 56.8%.

Looking at the valuation of the company, I don't see a prospect that's trading on the cheap. But I also don't see a firm that's drastically overvalued. At some point, market conditions will improve. But right now, we are in a downward trend. In light of that, I actually think that taking a more pessimistic approach is justified. And that is why I have decided, for this article, to downgrade the stock to a soft ‘sell.’

When it comes to investing, it is necessary to remain flexible, adjusting your opinion to reflect changes in data. It just so happens that new data is around the corner. Before the market opens on February 5th, the management team at AGCO Corporation is expected to announce financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. But leading up to that point, analysts are not very enthusiastic. Although profitability is forecasted to improve, revenue is expected to drop. And based on the overall trajectory that the company has taken, that's not surprising to me. So even though I could change my mind, it would take a pretty significant improvement that's unexpected in order to do that.

Industry Woes

In the event that you are new to AGCO Corporation, it's helpful to note exactly what the company does. In essence, the company provides a branded portfolio of products to its customers, which includes not only farmers but also OEMs. Examples here include tractors of all levels of horsepower, ranging from those under 40 to those as high as 650, as well as combines, hay tools, forage equipment, planters, certain application equipment used for things like crop protection, replacement parts, and even grain storage and protein production systems.

Truly, AGCO Corporation is a play on the agricultural sector. And on top of this, it's worth noting that the business is incredibly innovative. In 2024, for instance, the company launched PTx, which is an entirely new brand dedicated to the precision agricultural industry. The technologies developed here help farms to improve profitability and sustainability at the same time, and the hope that management has is that this line of business will grow nicely as time goes on.

Unfortunately, recent financial performance has been disappointing. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue from 2023 to 2024 plummeted from $14.41 billion to $11.66 billion. This drop of 19.1% was driven mostly by lower sales volumes that management attributed to soft industry sales as end market demand plummeted and as the company was negatively impacted by foreign currency fluctuations.

There is some interesting data regarding this segment of the economy. Sticking to the US, the USDA said that net farm income in 2024 was $127.8 billion. This was down from the $147.3 billion reported for 2023 and was down from the $182 billion reported the year before that. The good news is that net farm income was forecast to increase to $179.8 billion last year. But the bad news is that this was not in the areas that would have really helped a firm such as AGCO Corporation. For starters, a big portion of the increase came from government funding. In fact, due to a bill signed by President Joe Biden shortly before leaving office, government spending in the sector increased from $10.1 billion in 2024 to $40.5 billion last year.

Even without this, there was an increase in cash receipts from $511.3 billion to $535.2 billion. However, this was really because of animal and related products revenue jumping from $268.6 billion to $298.6 billion. Cattle, for instance, proved exceptionally profitable, driven by supply constraints. But on the other hand, crops actually dropped from $242.7 billion to $236.6 billion. For context, back in 2022, cash receipts for crops amounted to an all-time high of $282.7 billion.

As revenue drops, you should expect profitability to take a hit. Net income went from $1.17 billion in 2023 to negative $424.8 million in 2024. Cash flows declined similarly, as the first chart in this article illustrates. And sadly, pain has largely continued into 2025. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first nine months of that year, with revenue of $7.16 billion falling well short of the $8.77 billion the company reported the previous year. According to management, this drop in revenue was driven overwhelmingly by weakness in North America, with sales plummeting 33.1% from $1.79 billion to $1.20 billion. That drop was caused by lower sales volumes, most notably when it came to high-horsepower tractors, sprayers, and combines. However, there was weakness elsewhere, including in the Middle East and Europe, the Asia Pacific region, and South America. So North America, while especially painful, is not the only part of the world experiencing difficulties right now.

We did see profitability improve massively from negative $169.1 million to positive $631 million. However, this was only because of the absence of the $497.8 million associated with the loss of the sale of business operations that occurred in 2024. There were other changes as well, but that was the largest impact. Operating cash flow did improve from negative $108 million to positive $224.5 million. But if we strip out changes in working capital, we get a sharp decline from $553.1 million to $304.4 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business declined from $928.4 million to $653.2 million. In all likelihood, the rest of 2025 ended up painful as well. Management said that in North America, tractor sales were down 10% while combined sales were down 29%. In Western Europe, these drops were 8% and 7%, respectively. And in Brazil, tractor sales were up 3% while combined sales were down 10%. A continuation of this pointed toward revenue for 2025 of $9.8 billion. And with that, adjusted operating cash flow would have been on track to drop to $363.5 million while EBITDA would have fallen to $881.9 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These multiples certainly don't put the company in the fair value category, especially in light of broader economic weakness. But they don't put the company in the overvalued category on their own. Relative to other similar firms, the stock is decently priced. In that same chart, I compared the business to five other firms that are similar to it. Three were cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, while one was cheaper when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will be announcing results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year before the market opens on February 5th. I wish I could say that I had some good news when it came to revenue. However, analysts currently believe that sales will amount to only $2.67 billion. That would represent a pretty substantial drop from the $2.89 billion the company reported a year earlier. The good news is that earnings per share are forecasted to improve from a loss of $3.42 in 2024 to a profit of $1.74 in 2025. That would mean a rise in net income from negative $255.7 million to positive $130 million. But again, one-time events like impairment charges and losses on the sale of assets can play a role here. More likely than not, the lower revenue will translate to lower cash flows as well, and in the table below, you can see what those were for the final quarter of 2024.

Given all the data available, I would normally take a more neutral stance when it comes to a firm such as AGCO Corporation. However, industry conditions are expected to worsen. For the US agricultural industry, for instance, one forecast called for exports for 2026 of only $169 billion. That compares to the $220 billion reported a year earlier. It appears as though trade-related disputes are causing this, with China in particular cutting back on purchases of soybeans. Considering that 24% of the company's revenue came from North America in 2024, that alone could have a pretty big impact on the business. Europe, on the other hand, is even larger, accounting for 55% of revenue during that year. Forecasts for Europe specifically are difficult to come by. But according to one source, between 2026 and 2035, agricultural output is expected to grow at a rate of about 0.8% per year. But given the recent weakness that we have had, I would be surprised if we don't see some disruptions.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that the best thing that can be done is to take a more pessimistic stance on AGCO Corporation right now. As interesting as the company is, I think that further weakening is on the horizon. At some point, things will bottom out, and the firm will become attractively priced again. But for now, I think that downgrading it to a soft ‘sell’ is the right choice.

data.title: CRE Transaction Volumes Jump With And Without Data Centers
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

U.S. commercial real estate (CRE) transaction volumes rose 12% in December 2025 ($71.4bn) compared to the same month last year. Fourth quarter volumes reached $185.8bn, up 30% versus last year, while full year 2025 transactions increased 23% to $545.3bn. This marks the second consecutive year of rising deal activity (after 14% growth in 2024), pointing to a continued thaw in market liquidity after the prior downturn.

Reported volumes were meaningfully boosted by a sizable forward sale (~$23bn) of a data center under construction. Importantly, even excluding data centers in both 2025 and 2024, investment volumes still rose 14% in 4Q and 19% for the full year. This reflects a broad-based rebound in deal activity across every major traditional property type:

Property 4Q25 2025
All 30% 23%
All (Ex-Data Centers) 14% 19%
Office 23% 26%
Industrial 3% 15%
Retail 31% 26%
Apartment 4% 9%
Hotel 57% 13%
Land 25% 44%
Senior Housing 22% 47%
Data Centers 542% 274%

Source: MSCI, Real Capital Analytics, Principal Real Estate. Data as of 4Q 2025.

Bottom line

Transaction volumes are a useful barometer of market sentiment: accelerating activity typically reflects rising demand and confidence, while decelerating volumes point to growing caution. The market continues to face meaningful risks and uncertainties, but the recent pickup in transaction activity, reminiscent of the post-GFC recovery, is notable. It suggests the market may be reaching a similar inflection point, marked by returning confidence and renewed investor engagement.

CRE transaction volume

2022–present

This backdrop is consistent with the 2026 Global CRE Outlook: A Cycle for Selectivity, which argues that commercial real estate has entered a recovery phase by most traditional measures. However, returns are diverging sharply across sectors, regions, and fund strategies. This unevenness isn’t a flaw in the cycle—it is the cycle. Investors are no longer confronting a broad-based downturn; they are navigating widening dispersion. As the cycle ahead will likely be alpha-driven, real estate investors will need to take a page from equity investors’ playbook: asset and market selection will drive performance.

Original Post

data.title: A Municipal Investor's Guide To Understanding Infrastructure Financing
data.description:
Brightline East and West show that some munis depend more on the cash flow of a single project. Learn how to spot risk factors and make smarter infrastructure investing decisions.

What is Project Finance?

Project finance is a way of funding infrastructure where bond repayment depends mainly on the performance of a single project, not on a government’s tax revenues. Investors are paid from the project’s own cash flow and liquidity, which means factors like execution, operating results, and access to refinancing play a central role. When municipal bonds are structured this way, they behave less like traditional munis and more like stand-alone businesses that must manage cash carefully to survive changing market conditions.

Brightline East and Brightline West: Municipal Bonds Backed by One Project’s Cash Flow

Municipal investors are used to sorting credits by familiar labels: general obligation, essential service revenue, appropriation, and the rest. Brightline is a useful reminder that another category exists inside the tax-exempt market: private, single-asset infrastructure financed with private activity bonds, where the ultimate driver of outcomes is not a tax base but enterprise cash flow, liquidity, and access to refinancing.

That distinction matters because Brightline’s story has shifted. It’s no longer just about building rails and growing ridership. It’s also about capital structure, amendments, exchange mechanics, and how stakeholders allocate pain (and upside) when the original plan collides with the reality of higher rates, slower ramps, cost increases and tighter risk tolerance.

Brightline East vs West: Munis in Focus

  • Brightline Florida (often casually called Brightline East) is a completed high-speed intercity passenger rail currently connecting Miami and Orlando, with connections in between. The underwriting question for its senior bonds is fundamentally an operating one: can ridership, pricing power, and cost control translate into durable debt service capacity and enough liquidity to withstand volatility without repeatedly returning to the market on disadvantageous terms?
  • Brightline West is a planned greenfield high-speed rail system connecting Las Vegas and Southern California. Its underwriting question is fundamentally a development one: can the project execute construction on time and on budget, assemble a resilient capital stack, and convert a megaproject plan into an operating business without overleveraging the ramp?

If you approach both as “infrastructure munis,” you’ll miss what actually drives spread, ratings pressure and restructuring risk.

Why Brightline’s Municipal Bonds Started Trading Like Project Finance

Project finance credits often look fine until a specific combination of conditions hits: higher rates, weak liquidity, cost increases and a maturity wall that forces refinancing at the exact wrong time. When that happens, the conversation quickly shifts from long-term ridership potential to near-term survival: covenant headroom, collateral packages, intercreditor dynamics, and sponsor support. That shift has been visible in Brightline’s recent debt actions.

Brightline Florida: From Growth to Liability Management

Over the past year, Brightline Florida’s capital structure has shown the classic signs of a project-finance issuer managing through a constrained window.

First, the subordinated level of the stack demonstrated real flexibility in form but stress in substance. Brightline deferred interest payments in both July 2025 and January 2026 on a large tranche of high coupon, subordinated tax-exempt bonds. The ability to defer is part of how some of these securities are designed, but the act of using that feature is a meaningful signal: when cash is scarce, the subordinated layer becomes a pressure valve.

Second, a near-term payment wall forces a market-facing solution. Brightline Florida senior bonds were initially structured with what most people considered substantial reserves, allowing it a perceived adequate timeframe to ramp up its business to sustainable, recurring levels.

Those reserves are now expected to be fully used to help pay debt service in 2026, leaving the interest payment due in January of 2027 in question without a meaningful uplift in operations. This has forced Brightline to explore the issuance of up to $100 million in additional debt to help bolster liquidity, in part. Further, the subordinated tax-exempt Brightline Florida bonds have the ability to defer interest payments up to three times without triggering an event of default.

Although this provides better internal liquidity in the interim, it will also increase the total interest cost for this tranche, and the question remains whether Brightline will be able to pay these bonds going forward without an external equity contribution.

For muni investors, that’s the point where “tax-exempt” stops being the headline and “liquidity risk” becomes the headline. A higher coupon and a short fuse on upcoming payments can keep the lights on, but it also hardwires a new deadline into the credit story.

The practical takeaway for Florida is that operating momentum matters, but it must be judged through the lens of liquidity and upcoming checkpoints. If monthly ridership and revenue are improving, the question becomes whether that improvement is sufficient to rebuild cash reserves and reduce the need for repeated high-cost payment maneuvers.

In this kind of structure, “good news” is not a record month; “good news” is a credible path to sustainable coverage plus liquidity that’s large enough to make the next refinancing optional rather than mandatory.

Brightline West: Restructuring to a New Muni Bond Structure

Brightline West’s recent developments are even more explicit: the bonds were recently restructured while the project is still being financed and built.

A transaction support agreement established the framework for a bond exchange designed to transition existing holders to a new bond structure through a combination of both private and public processes. The substance of the deal provided a window into what creditors are demanding at this stage of the project:

  • A new senior secured bond series that sits ahead of legacy bonds that did not participate.
  • A meaningful repurchase of prior bonds for participating holders, reducing outstanding debt.
  • Warrants that allow creditors potential equity participation.
  • A timed equity-raise commitment, with staged injections and an earmark to redeem part of the new issuance.

Stepping back, that’s a specific template: de-risk the stack by moving up in seniority, reduce leverage via repurchase, and secure upside participation via warrants while requiring fresh equity to prove sponsor commitment.

For muni investors, this is the cleanest way to understand Brightline West right now: it’s not yet a ridership story. It’s a capital formation story. The primary risk factors are cost inflation, schedule slippage, and funding durability. A well-structured exchange can buy time and reset incentives, but it also tells you that the original structure no longer fits the market’s tolerance.

What Infrastructure Investors Should Know About Brightline Muni Bonds

Brightline is not an argument against passenger rail. It’s an argument for precise underwriting.

These credits can belong in a muni portfolio, but they should be treated more like single-asset, user-fee project finance than like traditional essential-service revenue bonds. That means investors should watch different things and interpret events differently.

Checklist for Evaluating Infrastructure Municipal Bonds

Key Risk Indicators for Brightline Florida:

  • Liquidity trajectory: cash reserves and the likelihood of additional deferrals or additional debt raises.
  • Pricing and mix: whether revenue quality is improving, not just passenger counts.
  • Operating leverage: how incremental revenue converts to operating cash flow after costs.
  • Sponsor behavior: evidence of equity support or other actions that permanently improve flexibility rather than merely extending the runway.

Key Risk Indicators for Brightline West:

  • Funding stack durability: how grants, bonds, loans, and equity fit together over time.
  • Cost and schedule discipline: whether scope and contingency planning keep the project financeable through completion.
  • Equity timing: whether commitments are translated into cash on the dates that matter.

How Diversified Muni ETFs Help Manage Risk in a Changing Market

Brightline East and West are a case study in the evolution of “muni” toward infrastructure project finance. The tax-exempt label may reduce the cost of capital at the margin, but it doesn’t change the underlying physics: user-fee assets live and die by liquidity, execution, and refinancing access. In a higher-rate world, capital structure is not a footnote to the story. It is the story.

Brightline doesn’t mean infrastructure is un-investable, it means labels can be misleading. When “muni” is really project finance, investors should demand a different checklist: liquidity runway, refinancing optionality, construction and funding durability, and evidence of sponsor support that’s real, not rhetorical.

The broader takeaway is that you don’t have to swing at every high-spread story to use munis effectively. VanEck’s suite of municipal bond ETFs provides purposeful muni exposure, anchoring portfolios with diversified core allocations. Targeted exposures can then be added when the risk/reward is truly compensating, so the after-tax income story isn’t dependent on the next amendment, exchange, or maturity wall.

IMPORTANT DISCLOSURES

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. data.title: AMD's 2026 An EPYC Year - MI450x Not The Only Tailwind
data.description:
Investment Thesis

2025 correctly echoed the strong bullishness in Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD). I had been vociferously advocating for it over the past 18 months.

AMD was initially caught flat-footed in the AI arms race to deploy GPUs for rapidly proliferating AI & HPC workloads. But in typical dark-horse fashion that AMD is now accustomed to, the company seems to have gotten its GPU strategy locked in, winning a growing number of GPU deployments in AI data centers.

And I strongly believe the company’s strategy to win more chip deployments in the data center will break more glass ceilings this year. That’s because AMD’s MI450x GPUs, made for rack-scale deployments, finally start shipping in H2 this year, with a third major hyperscaler reportedly set on deploying AMD’s MI455 GPUs. I also believe AMD’s EPYC line of CPUs might surprise every investor on the upside, with strong CPU shipments expected.

The combination of the MI455 GPU & EPYC CPUs is potent enough for AMD to continue its acceleration through CY26, leading me to reiterate my strong bullishness on AMD’s shares.

AMD Sailing In Twin Tailwind Mode This Year

Most of my prior research about AMD was focused on addressing the skepticism in AMD’s DC (data center) business accelerating towards the 50-60% growth rates that its larger peers, Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO), have committed to.

AMD has come a long way since initially stumbling on their GPU ambitions in the DC buildout by working with industry analysts and executing on a sharp GPU strategy that positioned AMD’s GPUs very well for the inference boom that the industry is currently in the early innings of.

In a recent post about Sandisk (SNDK) I explain in detail as to why I believe the inference boom is playing out and how expanding context windows in AI models has been fueling the need for more memory.

This is one of the core macro technology transitions in the GPU end market for DCs that AMD got right last year, delivering data center-focused Instinct GPUs that boasted far superior memory capacity and memory bandwidth. Almost every GPU that AMD has launched last year has boasted memory of ~2x Nvidia’s GPUs. The upcoming MI400 line of Instinct GPUs is expected to have HBM4 memory of 432GB, well over the 288GB, while the MI400’s memory bandwidth will also significantly increase to 19.6Tb/s, more than 2x the 8 TB/s of the MI350 series.

Adding to AMD’s product-winning strategy, the company’s ability to make targeted acquisitions, and the adoption of popular DC architectures like UltraEthernet and ESUN, the company suddenly arrived with an extremely compelling rack-scale solution, Helios, for AI DCs, which found immediate appeal among the hyperscaler target market.

As it stands, AMD now has Oracle (ORCL) and OpenAI (OPENAI) lined up as major clients, with Meta Platforms (META) reportedly the third major client for AMD’s cutting-edge chips and rack-scale solutions. With these developments, consensus revenue estimates have been slowly rising and seem to be penciling in ~32% growth in CY25 and CY26, with acceleration projected ahead to 38% growth in CY27 to $62B in revenues. Currently, AMD’s Data Center segment accounts for just under 50% of the company’s consolidated revenue base, but with the kind of growth expected, AMD’s DC segment revenue will account for >50% share starting in CY25.

In my strong observation, I believe markets are still being pessimistic regarding AMD’s 32% revenue growth outlook for this year. That’s because AMD had already unveiled at a Financial Analyst Day late last year that the company would be targeting:

  1. >35% revenue growth rates
  2. adj. operating margins of >35% and,
  3. adj. EPS >$20

Forget the operating margins and EPS targets for a few moments and observe the >300 bp difference between the market’s 32% top-line expectations this year and AMD’s >35% top-line growth projections starting this year. The 300 bp difference is a meaningful delta that will boost AMD’s valuation multiples, per my estimates.

Also, in my view, analysts are underappreciating a major tech tailwind that can further boost AMD’s outlook starting this year.

CPUs are expected to have a major comeback this year as DC architectural complexity increases amid the significant ramp-up in GPU density on the same network fabric. At the analyst day, an AMD exec unpacked the early signs for the EPYC CPU’s demand signals:

The CPU is now playing even a bigger role in inference because it's a cost-effective way to infer on a smaller model. It also is in a mixed workload environment because CPUs actually have multiple jobs with our high core counts leveraging the extra core counts there for inference, real-time inference has gone extremely well.

As of Q2 CY25, AMD commanded a 40% hold over CPU shipments for the DC market, as seen in the slide above. The company is targeting a 50% market share, and they believe their EPYC CPU will get them there.

At CES, three weeks ago, AMD’s Lisa Su soft-launched the latest Turin line of EPYC CPUs, reportedly AMD’s highest-ever core count CPU. High core counts significantly improve multi-tasking which is one of the key mainstay features for AMD’s DC CPUs over rivals. This should enable the company to extend its market share to its 50% target.

This sell-side analyst made a powerful observation regarding Intel’s (INTC) server sales, from Intel’s filings, which could further boost AMD’s sales growth and margin aspirations specifically for CPUs, in my opinion:

Additionally, Rakers said the 10-K disclosed that 2025 server CPU volume grew 9% year-over-year. However, the average selling price fell 4%, due in part to pricing actions taken in the first-half of the year and a higher mix of “lower core count products,” Rakers added. As such, he believes that fourth-quarter shipments of server CPUs grew 8% year-over-year, down from 13% growth in the second-quarter and flat in the third quarter.

I suspect markets haven’t yet fully woken up to the realization of how important AMD’s EPYC can be for the company’s DC aspirations, and that is reflected in the valuation multiples.

AMD’s Valuation Multiples Might Be Rich But Are Lagging

As AMD heads into its earnings next week, the company’s shares are trading at 38x CY26 EPS, expected to grow a whopping 65%, or 9x CY26 sales of $45B, growing 32.6%.

There are two things that I mentioned earlier in this post. First, I believe analysts are not fully accounting for the boost in AMD’s EPYC sales. Second, the 32% consensus for top-line growth in the markets is still falling short of the >35% top-line growth target that AMD has set for itself. Therefore, in my opinion, there exists a meaningful amount of pessimism in the valuation levels and the consensus estimates.

I am going to hold off on estimating price targets for AMD right before next week’s earnings report, but I believe in being ultra bullish on AMD as I write this report out for investors. I do find AMD’s 9x forward sales multiple to be very attractive.

Risks & What To Watch For In Q4 ER

Among AMD’s large clients, Oracle and OpenAI are two of the clients for AMD’s chips, which possibly are central to a large part of AMD’s forward growth. By H2 CY26, AMD’s moment of truth arrives when the company is expected to supply the MI400 line of GPUs when Oracle’s and OpenAI’s bills come due. Investors can expect meaningful periods of volatility in shares through H2 CY26. I also expect AMD’s management to share more details on their confidence in these two clients in next week’s call.

What I’m expecting in the Q4 call:

  • AMD does not break out its DC revenue by CPU/GPU, but for CY25 I am expecting >$16B in DC revenue. At a company level, AMD’s CY25 revenue estimates are ~$34B, implying 32% growth, while CY25 EPS of $4 per share implies 20% growth.
  • In CY26, markets are expecting EPS of $6.57 per share, implying significant acceleration of 65% growth, on revenues worth $45B, implying 32.6% growth.
  • AMD may not reveal full year projections but I fully expect management to publish Q1 CY26 projections. Expectations are EPS of $1.22, 27% growth on revenues of $9.4B, 26% growth.

If AMD’s management can hint at any sort of forward supply being sold out, it will spark a rally in the company’s shares. I say this because there are certain reports that speculate general servers and CPU 2026 volumes are “already sold out.”

Takeaway

The sharp and sustained performance in AMD that I expect this year is not just going to come from its GPU business. With AI inference booming and server architecture getting complex, demand for CPUs will add another layer to AMD’s tailwinds.

I reiterate my strong bullishness on AMD’s shares ahead of the company’s earnings next week.
